module.title: Sound Design Fundamentals
module.description: Learn to shape sound from first principles — oscillators, filters, envelopes, FM synthesis — so you can design exactly the sound you hear in your head rather than settling for what a preset gives you.

lessons.title: Understanding Oscillators
lessons.body:
Every synthesised sound begins with an oscillator — an electronic circuit (or its software equivalent) that produces a repeating waveform at a set frequency. The frequency determines pitch. The *shape* of the waveform determines timbre: what the sound actually sounds like beyond its pitch.

Understanding the four basic waveforms is the single most useful thing you can learn before touching a synthesiser. Once you can hear a waveform and know which one it is, sound design stops being guesswork.

## The Four Basic Waveforms

**Sine wave** — the purest sound possible. A sine wave contains only the fundamental frequency and no harmonics at all. It sounds smooth, round, and hollow — think of a tuning fork or a pure whistle. Because it has no harmonic content, it sits cleanly in a mix without interfering with other sounds. Sines are the go-to for sub-bass (below 80 Hz) and for creating soft, airy tones.

**Triangle wave** — like a sine with a small number of odd harmonics added. It sounds slightly brighter and more present than a sine but still retains a soft, rounded quality. Useful for flute-like leads, soft keys sounds, and anywhere you want a sine but with a little more character.

**Square wave** — contains only odd harmonics (the 3rd, 5th, 7th partial, and so on), each progressively quieter. It sounds hollow and reedy — not unlike a clarinet, which also emphasises odd harmonics due to its cylindrical bore. Square waves work well for vintage-style leads, organ emulations, and chiptune sounds. At lower pitches they take on a thick, buzzy quality.

**Sawtooth wave** — contains *all* harmonics, both odd and even, diminishing in a consistent pattern. This makes it the brightest and richest of the four shapes. A sawtooth sounds like a buzzing, brash tone — imagine a bowed string or a brass instrument. Because it has the most harmonic content, it gives a filter the most material to work with, which is why it is the default starting point for most subtractive synthesis patches.

## Frequency and Pitch

Oscillators are tuned in two units:

- **Semitones** (coarse tuning) — one semitone is one piano key. Tuning two oscillators apart by a musical interval (a fifth = 7 semitones, an octave = 12 semitones) creates harmonic richness or deliberate dissonance.
- **Cents** (fine tuning) — 100 cents = 1 semitone. Detuning two oscillators by a small number of cents (2–10) creates a subtle beating effect as their waveforms drift in and out of phase. This is what gives synthesised sounds their characteristic shimmer and width.

## Detune and Unison

**Detune** — offsetting two or more oscillators by a few cents from each other. The resulting interference pattern creates a chorus-like thickening. A single oscillator sounds static and synthetic; two slightly detuned sawtooth waves together sound alive.

**Unison** — stacking multiple copies of the same oscillator, each offset in pitch and/or phase. Four unison voices at ±10 cents produces a lush, wide sound often described as "supersaw" when using the sawtooth waveform. The more voices, the thicker — but also the less focused and punchier. Unison is used heavily in EDM leads and pads.

## Phase

Phase describes where in its cycle a waveform starts when a note is triggered. Most synthesisers let you set this to random (the oscillator starts at a random point each time you press a key) or fixed (it always starts at the same point). Fixed phase gives a consistent, punchy transient on attack — useful for leads and basses. Random phase gives a slightly different character each note — useful for pads and evolving sounds.
keyTakeaways: The four basic waveforms — sine, triangle, square, sawtooth — each have a distinct harmonic content that determines their tonal character before any filter or effect is applied. Sawtooth has the most harmonics; sine has none.
Detuning two oscillators by a few cents against each other creates a beating interference that makes synthesised sounds feel alive. This is the foundation of the 'supersaw' and most rich pad sounds.
Pitch is set in semitones (coarse) and cents (fine). Tuning oscillators a musical interval apart — a fifth (7 semitones), an octave (12) — adds harmonic richness intentionally rather than randomly.
lessons.tryThis: Open any synthesiser in FL Studio — 3xOsc works well for this. Set all three oscillators to the same pitch and cycle through each waveform shape one at a time: sine, triangle, square, sawtooth. Play the same note for each and just listen. Can you hear how each shape gets progressively brighter and more complex? Then set OSC1 and OSC2 both to sawtooth and detune OSC2 by +7 cents. Play a note. That shimmer you hear is the beating effect — that single change is responsible for most of the 'thickness' in modern synthesised sounds.

lessons.title: Subtractive Synthesis with 3xOsc
lessons.body:
Subtractive synthesis is the most common form of synthesis in popular music production. The name describes the method: you begin with a sound that is harmonically rich — full of frequencies — and then remove (subtract) parts of it using a filter until what remains is the sound you want.

This approach is intuitive once you understand it. A sculptor starts with a block of marble and removes material. A subtractive synthesiser starts with a waveform full of harmonics and removes frequencies.

## The Signal Chain

Subtractive synthesis follows a fixed path:

**Oscillator → Filter → Amplifier**

1. **Oscillator** — generates the raw waveform. Sawtooth is the most common starting point because it contains all harmonics, giving the filter the most material to shape.
2. **Filter** — removes or attenuates certain frequencies. A low-pass filter (the most common) cuts high frequencies and lets low ones through. The cutoff frequency controls where the cutting starts.
3. **Amplifier** — controls the overall volume, shaped by an envelope over time.

Each stage also has a **modulator** — usually an envelope or LFO — that changes its behaviour over time. A filter that opens up after a note is triggered sounds very different from one that stays in a fixed position.

## 3xOsc in FL Studio

3xOsc is FL Studio's built-in three-oscillator synthesiser. It is not the most powerful synth in the package, but it is the clearest demonstration of subtractive principles because it exposes all the fundamentals with no distractions.

To load 3xOsc: in the Channel Rack, right-click an empty area → **Add one** → **3xOsc**.

**The interface**: three rows (OSC 1, OSC 2, OSC 3). For each oscillator:
- **Waveform selector** (the row of shape icons at the left) — choose your waveform
- **Coarse tuning** (CRS) — semitones up or down
- **Fine tuning** (FINE) — cents up or down
- **Volume** knob — the relative level of this oscillator in the mix
- **Panning** knob — left/right position
- **Phase offset** (PHSE) — where in the cycle the oscillator starts

## Building a Thick Lead from Scratch

Here is a step-by-step patch that produces a classic thick synthesiser lead:

**Step 1 — Set the waveforms.** Set all three oscillators to sawtooth.

**Step 2 — Tune for width.** Leave OSC 1 at 0 cents. Tune OSC 2 to +7 cents and OSC 3 to −7 cents. This spread creates a wide, shimmering sound.

**Step 3 — Add an octave layer.** Tune OSC 3 up 12 semitones (one octave) in addition to the detune. Now OSC 3 reinforces the fundamental with an octave-up layer, adding brightness and presence.

**Step 4 — Apply the filter.** Click the channel name in the Channel Rack to open 3xOsc, then click the **ENV/LFO** tab. Switch to the **FILTER** section. Set the filter type to **Low-pass**. Pull the cutoff down to around 40–50%. Add a small amount of resonance (Q). You should immediately hear the sound become darker and more focused.

**Step 5 — Shape the amplitude envelope.** In the same panel, set the **VOL** envelope: Attack to 0 (instant), Decay to a short value, Sustain to 80%, Release to a short tail. The sound should now have an immediate attack and hold while the key is pressed.

## Building a Simple Sub Bass

For a sub bass, the approach is different — you want very few harmonics:

- Set OSC 1 to **sine wave**, OSC 2 and OSC 3 off (volume to zero)
- Leave all tuning at zero — no detune for a bass, you want it locked in pitch
- Keep the filter fully open (cutoff at 100%) — there is nothing to remove from a sine
- Set the volume envelope: Attack 0, Decay short, Sustain 100%, Release short

The result is a clean, pure sub-bass tone. To add mid-bass presence, bring OSC 2 back in at low volume with a square wave — this adds the upper harmonics that make the bass audible on small speakers.

## What Subtractive Synthesis Cannot Do

Subtractive synthesis works best for sounds that are smoothly evolving from rich to dark. It is less suited to sounds with inharmonic spectra — metallic, glassy, or bell-like tones where the partials are not in simple integer ratios. Those require FM or physical modelling. Knowing this boundary tells you when to reach for a different tool.
keyTakeaways: Subtractive synthesis follows one signal path: Oscillator → Filter → Amplifier. Start with a rich waveform (sawtooth), carve away frequencies with the filter, and shape the volume over time with an envelope.

Detuning 3xOsc's oscillators by a few cents (OSC2 at +7, OSC3 at −7) creates width and shimmer with no additional effects. Adding an octave-up layer on a third oscillator adds brightness and presence.

Sub bass: one sine oscillator, no detune, filter fully open. Lead: multiple detuned sawtooths, filter pulled back to taste. The waveform and filter position are the two most important decisions in any patch.
lessons.tryThis: Build the thick lead patch described above in 3xOsc: three sawtooth oscillators, OSC2 at +7 cents, OSC3 at −7 cents and +12 semitones. Play a few notes in the Piano Roll. Then open the filter section and slowly drag the cutoff from 100% down to 10% while the pattern loops. Listen to how the character changes as harmonics are removed. Notice where it sounds interesting to you — that is your filter cutoff position for this patch. Save it at the point you like best.
